[PUBLISHED DATE]: Unknown Date
[TITLE]: The environmental cost of abandoning your tent at a music festival
[METADATA/INTRO]: After years of depressing images of huge fields strewn with abandoned tents and rubbish in the aftermath of music festivals, it was heartening to hear Glastonbury Festival organisers claim that 99% of festival-goers’ tents were picked up after the festival....
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: After years of depressing images of huge fields strewn with abandoned tents and rubbish in the aftermath of music festivals, it was heartening to hear Glastonbury Festival organisers claim that 99% of festival-goers’ tents were picked up after the festival. For a festival of 200,000 people this is a huge achievement. But really it should be normal. Hopefully this bodes well for future years, because the Association of Independent Festivals estimates that a quarter of a million so called “single-use” tents are abandoned at music festivals across the UK each year. 
 There is a popular belief that most tents left behind after festivals are collected and sold by charities or sent to good causes. But this is wishful thinking: most of it end up in landfill. The practical and cost overheads for festival organisers and charities of collecting thousands of tents are just too much. All the elements of the tents – pegs, poles, sheets and bags – need to be present, undamaged, and the tent sufficiently clean and functional to be reused.

[PUBLISHED DATE]: 2025
[TITLE]: The Festival Hangover: Why Tents Left Behind Are an Environmental Disaster - Nouse
[METADATA/INTRO]: When the final act leaves the stage and the crowds begin their journeys home, festival fields across the UK transform almost overnight into fields of plastic. What was once a sea of people, music and joy becomes an overwhelming wave...
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: When the final act leaves the stage and the crowds begin their journeys home, festival fields across the UK transform almost overnight into fields of plastic. What was once a sea of people, music and joy becomes an overwhelming wave of abandoned tents, chairs and rubbish. Festivals like Leeds, Reading and Glastonbury may be well known for their music line-ups, but each year they also generate a less glamorous spectacle: thousands of tents left behind. 
 According to the Association of Independent Festivals (AIF), UK festivals see an estimated 250,000 tents abandoned each year. For festival goers, a Â£25 tent might feel like a bargain, but behind the convenience lies a serious and ever growing environmental problem.

[PUBLISHED DATE]: 2018-08-29
[TITLE]: Thousands of tents left behind at Reading Festival that will now go to landfill | Metro News
[METADATA/INTRO]: These pictures show the scale of the clean-up operation facing organisers of festivals around the country. Thousands of tents – many only used once – will now go to landfill because they can’t be re-used or given to charity....
[BEST EVIDENCE CHUNK]: These pictures show the scale of the clean-up operation facing organisers of festivals around the country. 
 Thousands of tents – many only used once – will now go to landfill because they can’t be re-used or given to charity. 
 These pictures show those that were left on the side of the River Thames at Reading Festival in Berkshire, but the pictures could be from any festival across the UK. 
 The cost of clearing away the detritus will come from ticket sales and festival organisers confirmed that most of the tents will be thrown away. 
 In some areas, huge heaps of already-dismantled £30 tents could be seen while an astonishing sea of litter, dumped drinks cans and cardboard food containers was just left across the huge site.
